# Meeting Summary for AI WG

**NOTICE**: This summary was auto-generated by Zoom's "AI". AI-generated
content may be inaccurate or misleading. Always check for accuracy. If in
doubt, please consult the meeting recording at
https://youtube.com/@GraphQLFoundation/playlists

- Meeting start: 2026-05-28T17:30:01Z
- Meeting end: 2026-05-28T18:42:26Z
- Summary start: 2026-05-28T17:30:01Z
- Summary end: 2026-05-28T18:42:24Z

The May 2026 GraphQL AI Working Group meeting focused on two main topics: AppMock specification updates and semantic introspection development. Mark provided an update on the AppMock specification, announcing that it has been published as a GAPS spec with operation and field level mocking capabilities, and reported interest from companies like Meta and potential implementation in Relay. Pascal then presented his work on semantic introspection, demonstrating a new approach that filters schema data based on user queries rather than using the traditional two-turn process of search followed by definitions. The discussion revealed challenges around implementation complexity, model dependencies, and the need for balancing comprehensive schema coverage with efficient token usage, with participants exploring various approaches including the potential separation of search and definition phases and the use of local versus frontier models for implementation.

## Next Steps

- Michael Rebello: Open a PR with the discussed changes to the mock spec this week.
- Mark: Send the Relay implementation of the mock spec to the group for review.
- Mark: Add a section and explicit demo for mocking mutations to the mock spec website.
- Benjie: Drive the pull request for allowing empty selection sets in the spec, to be discussed in the next working group meeting.
- Pascal: Post the Node.js implementation with all the semantic search strategies and parameters for others to try.
- All attendees: Add their names to the attendee list and ensure it gets merged.
- Interested parties: Apply for a GraphQL grant for AI working group contributions, and ping Kewei and Martin for help with the application.

## Summary

### Working Group Member Introductions

The meeting began with introductions from working group members, including Dale from Toronto working on Apollo MSP server and skills, Pascal from Switzerland, Mark from Austin, Jeff from Connecticut, Michael Rebello from Airbnb in San Diego, Martin from Paris at Apollo, Hugh from Meta in Menlo Park, and Steven from Netflix. The meeting was hosted by Kewei from Menlo Park, who noted that the agenda items were the same as previously proposed by Pascal. The session appeared to be focused on working group member introductions rather than specific agenda items or decisions.

### GraphQL Grants and Spec Updates

Kewei announced that GraphQL grants are still available and encouraged attendees to apply for projects related to blog posts, open source contributions, or GraphQL.js work, including potential contributions from the AI working group. Mark provided an update on the mock spec, noting that it has been published on GAPS and is open for implementation, with Apollo already having an implementation. Mark mentioned that Yelp plans to use the spec and aims to find a better home for the reference implementation, with potential interest from Meta and a Relay implementation in progress.

### GraphQL @mock Directive Implementation

The team discussed the implementation of the @mock directive in GraphQL, where Mark explained it works as a client-side transformation that removes mock selections before sending requests to the server. Pascal inquired about file-based mocking options, and Mark confirmed this approach exists for mocking entire subtrees using JSON files, particularly for LLM-generated responses. Benjie mentioned submitting a pull request to allow empty selection sets with @mock directives.

### GraphQL Framework Development Discussion

Mark discussed his GraphQL mocking framework and addressed questions from Hugh about using it for agent evaluation outside of web clients and handling mutations. The group debated whether the framework should depend on supporting empty selection sets, with Michael and Benjie explaining the implementation complexities and proposing to separate this behavior from the @mock directive. Pascal presented work on semantic introspection, demonstrating a new approach that filters schema data based on semantic relevance rather than using the original introspection method, though he noted challenges in determining what constitutes semantically meaningful schema elements.

### Semantic Search for GraphQL Schemas

Pascal presented his work on semantic search and semantic introspection for GraphQL schema exploration, demonstrating how his approach can reduce schema size and improve relevance by selectively fetching relevant parts of the schema based on user queries. The discussion revealed challenges with implementing this approach, including variations in results across different embedding models and the need to balance token efficiency with comprehensive schema coverage. The team agreed on the importance of having two separate steps: a search phase to identify relevant types and a definitions phase to retrieve field information, with Pascal planning to explore both local LLMs and frontier models to test the implementation across different capabilities.
